approach keyword research and assignment generation, even though that's not something that you might be doing on a day-to-day basis for your clients, but it does help with creating content.

keywords into high-quality assignments for clients.
And I'm going to give you a couple of tips and strategies how you can do it and how you can use AI to speed up the process but still get really good results.
At the top of the doc, there is, I think Marcel put this together, it's called Creating a Winning Content Strategy.
It's more of a high-level document, but I think it's still helpful to read, to understand his approach to content strategies.
So, helps to break down what the current practice at GrowthX is today.
So, depending on, I know that not all CMs have the same experience.
When I had my pod, I was doing keyword research and generating the assignments.
But I know that some CMs have also been asked to generate assignments and do keyword research.
And the current practice at GrowthX favors keyword gap analysis as a default.
So,
Well, this process is fairly straightforward.
We run a keyword gap analysis with the client's top competitors.
We extract the relevant URLs from that analysis.
We use the URLs to generate assignments, and then we do some pruning, but we upload those assignments into the content OS, and we send them to the clients for approval.
This approach is very valuable, but it's also limited because we over-rely on the competitors.
So, we only surface ideas that competitors have already published content on.
So, we might be missing dozens, if not hundreds, potential content ideas that the competitors haven't caught on to.
So, we're not trying to overtake them.
We're just trying to catch up.
But by doing a more comprehensive keyword research early on, we're going to not just catch up to them, but also overtake them.
And we're going to discover untapped opportunities.
And one thing that I've noticed with Atlas is...
Is that the URLs, depending on what they are, and depending on how well you prune them before generating the assignments, there can be a keyword intent mismatch.
So we end up targeting the wrong primary keyword because Atlas identifies the top-ranking keyword for a specific URL.
But that top-ranking keyword sometimes isn't the one that's most aligned with the assignment's actual intent.
So a client might look at an assignment and see that we are suggesting, I don't know, a guide about a specific topic.
But then they look at the primary keyword, and the primary keyword, when they Google it, it's a commercial keyword.
And it has, and so they, right off the bat, they're confused.
And if they have someone on their team who is SEO savvy, they're going to flag that and be like, hey, this isn't the right primary keyword.
So right off the bat, we're not building trust with them because they don't trust our judgment when it comes to identifying the right assignments.

And so the core issues with this approach, even though it's valuable and it absolutely should stay within the strategy research part, but the core issues are that we missed out on valuable keywords, so we limit our creativity, we overlook original angles, and there's a risk of stagnating the client's differentiation in the market.
And also, obviously, search intent is incredibly important.
If you don't meet the search intent, then all of the effort is going to be in vain.
Assignments won't rank, and we pay this, like, the consequences aren't immediate, but we will feel them in six to eight weeks when clients aren't happy with their results.
And we're going to have to go back and have to refresh a bunch of articles that we wouldn't have to refresh had we positioned them accurately in the first place.
So those are really the top issues when it comes to using just a keyword gap analysis.
Another thing is there is, and this is also how I understand this has to do with volumes, because we do want to identify like many assignments at once.
But if we don't pay attention to pruning the URL list early on, then we are going to have to do that at some point.
So the easiest way is to really very carefully look at the URL list and ask like, okay, is this assignment that a competitor has published, is this a good assignment for our client?
Is there a mismatch between their product and the client's product?

And when you spend some extra time analyzing the URLs in the beginning, it's going to save you a ton of time on analyzing the assignments later on, because you're just going to have to like, You're basically gonna have to check the titles.
And that's
So you're not going to be deleting assignments and doing like a full analysis.
So what other approach do I recommend?
And this isn't new, like everybody does this.
So this isn't anything original, but I want us to approach keyword research with the understanding that this process cannot be fully automated.
This is a very creative process.
It's messy.
It involves like research, creative thinking, trying to put yourself in the audience's shoes.
And this requires you to kind of sometimes think outside the box, spend some time really researching before diving into the keyword research and in the assignment generation process.
Specifically, you know, we want to ask what are the audience's daily frustrations?
is you questions.
What problems are they trying to solve?
Where do they spend their time?
What kind of websites do they visit?
What are the newsletters that they're subscribed to?
Like, do they read any subreddits?
All of those things.
And we could use all of that information to find search queries that would be very interesting to them.
And that could be very valuable for our customer as well, for our client as well.
So, the purpose of this keyword research really is to map the keywords that we find to the pain points of the customer or the audience.
There's also, like, a client experience factor to all of this.
So, right now, all of the clients, they do go through a strategy sprint.
And so, when you do get new clients, they will have already been with us for six to eight weeks.
But it's important to know that the content OS is one of the...
First Impressions, that's one of the first deliverables that they get from us.
So the first deliverable is usually the artifacts, which is the company context, the audience personas, and the writing guidelines.
After that comes the content OS.
If that content OS doesn't look good, if the assignments aren't good, they're going to be really disappointed.
And I'm kind of probably repeating myself already, but it's so important to deliver.
We don't have to deliver 150 assignments at once.
If we deliver just 50 really good ones, that they're like, this is spot on.
I love this.
They're going to feel so optimistic, so excited to start working with us.
And that's one of the most important reasons why we should get keyword research and assignment generation right.
And we really just don't want the client to feel like they, like we don't get them.
Because clients, by default, they might think, you know, nobody can understand their industry.
just.
And it's true to an extent, but we do want to show that we have put in the effort to just understand their industry and their audience.
So there are a couple of steps that go into really good keyword research, and it starts with understanding the client's goals.
There are a couple of documents that can help you here.
So you can start with the kickoff call.
The kickoff call is when they, when we really go deeply into what their current challenges are, what they're trying to do with content, what has worked for them in the past.
And the client's goals should always be the first input.
And if you have a longstanding client, you should still revisit, like, their goals change, sometimes on a quarterly basis.
So it's still worth, like, checking with them, hey, is this still something that you are striving towards?
Many of our recent clients have a very common goal.
Which is to drive qualified organic traffic or increase leads.
So the focus is automatically going to be on like bottom of the funnel and middle of the funnel content.
So more niche keywords, very kind of specific assignments.
That's going to be our target right off the bat.
So even before you started any research, you know that generic like what is content, just you can skip that like immediately.
And then the next step is to really familiarize yourself with the target audience.
Most clients have multiple audiences and it's our job to understand who is the priority.
If there are multiple priorities, we also have to work around that.
Sometimes some clients, we have a recent, a new strategy sprint client monograph who they target architects and engineers.
But...
They really want to focus more on engineers right now because they have underserved them with content previously.
So that's also another way you can filter assignments immediately just by, you know, inquiring about their audience priorities.
And it's also good to ask, like, does this persona even look for information online and how do they look for it?
Because someone who's a CEO isn't going to be researching the same way that someone who's, you know, middle management.
So there's a lot of differences in that approach.

And sometimes, you know, for some audiences, like if it's an executive or someone who's very high up in a company or especially in an enterprise, you know, you might come up with a different approach than standard blog posts.
So it's always worth, like, really spending some time there.
After this, want to, once you've identified the most.
Important audiences, you need to dig into their pain points.
So that means understanding what frustrates them, what problems they're trying to solve, what industry topics they're interested in.
And this helps us not just identify the right assignments, but we can address those pain points effectively in the content by introducing the client as a natural solution.
And we can also identify content that's adjacent to the client's product.

So even if, for example, like we have a client, Tiro, they're a dental staffing platform for dental hygienists specifically, but we have also created additional content targeting, like hiring dental assistants, because they know that dental offices are also going to be hiring dental assistants.
So they want to get in front of them because they just want to raise brand awareness any way that they can.
So you also want to like consider other content ideas that aren't specific to
product.
But these types of ideas are typically going to be lower priority than keywords that are related to their product.
The way you can use AI to make this process faster and a lot better is to expedite the research.
I come from a time of content marketing, like I started working as a content marketer before AI.

So this process used to take me a week, sometimes two weeks, because you would have to manually, like I had my very first, I'll never forget my very first client when I started working at a content marketing agency was a fintech company that created a payroll API that would pull income data from people's payroll platforms.
And like I had come in, like I was working at a content farm, like creating five articles.
per week that were at least 3,000 words long every day about motherhood and pregnancy.
I had, I did not know anything about fintech.
I did not know anything about APIs.
And this was like, you basically would have to start from scratch.
Like you would start reading like different fintech websites.
I was listening to podcasts to figure out like what the industry people were talking about, like what were the main challenges, were the main issues.
And then you would start from there.
And to be honest, like you would still miss key, a lot of key insights.
So you would have to talk to people who, or you would have to rely on the client a lot to provide like insights into what's important to their audience.
So that's, you know, that was the old way.
And now using like ChatGPT or Claude and perplexity, can, like in like an hour, you can get a pretty good understanding of their audience.
What you can do, and this is a process that you can absolutely
augment.
You can feature GPT or Claude with everything you know about the client.
So the artifacts, any internal documentation, any perplexity research that you've done.
And then ask AI to provide a comprehensive list of pain points by persona.
You can ask it to categorize the pain points by relevance so that you can kind of see them depending on different, you know, different categories.
And then you can ask it to generate seed keyword suggestions based on those pain points.
I say seed keywords because ChatGPT doesn't actually have access to a keyword database like SEMrush or Ahrefs.
So it's making stuff up.
And seed keywords are always better to use because there's a higher likelihood that those keywords actually exist rather than like a long tail keyword that doesn't exist that ChatGPT just made up.
So once you have that list of seed seed keywords.
So you can start using SEMrush and Google to find the actual keywords.
So you can use SEMrush's keyword magic tool to start finding different variations.
If no results show up, you can like Google the keyword to see what's ranking.
If something exists on that topic, then you can take the top ranking content, put it in domain overview in SEMrush, and see what are the keywords that that domain rank, that that URL ranks for.
So that can take you to a relevant keyword.
So you kind of have to be really crafty about it and like be willing to spend some time with each seed keyword to make sure that it works.
You can try simplified or related variants.
Or just, you know, if a seed keyword doesn't work out, you might be like, okay, what is kind of similar to this that I can try?
And if you, whenever you feel like.

Like, you're not, like, if you feel like you're missing something, you can always just go back to ChatGPT and be like, okay, for this persona, is there anything, you know, can you give me some more information that would improve my understanding of their day-to-day job?
Like, are there different, for example, for dental hygienists, like, are there different types of dental hygienists, like, that deal with, I don't know, if they're pediatric or whatever.
So, you know, you're just trying to expand your understanding of this persona and their job and, like, how they function every day.
So, you have to kind of take a broader approach here.
The truth is, like, this does take time.
And it should take time because it's one of the most, like, critical layers of our content strategies.
You should, you know, if you can find, like, after a day or two of doing the research, if you can find, like, 40 or 50.
really good assignments.

So we shouldn't be afraid to spend some time here because this is like, this is the first step of the assignment generation process.

You're not going to deliver content that the client doesn't like and then they backlog it.
You know, like there's nothing worse than like spending hours drafting a piece and then the client comes back to, it's like, actually, this isn't relevant for our industry.
So we want to minimize those instances as much as possible.
We also always want to prioritize the service.

search intent and I know a lot of you are probably like you already know what search intent is but it's the reason why someone goes to Google or to an LLM to answer a specific question and for blog content we're always looking at informational intent and commercial intent sometimes but navigational and transactional is out of the picture and this means to check the search intent you always have to you can check it it in SEMrush if SEMrush has the data but the best way to check the search intent you just scroll down in SEMrush's keyword overview and there's a SERP overview section and you can click on a link that will take you actually let me show you right now so if you scroll down click view SERP and you're going to see what the SERP looks like and and
You can just take a look at the top-ranking content, and you can already see this is all informational content.

There are no landing pages here, so the intent is clearly not commercial, and I cannot emphasize more the importance of checking the SERP before you generate an assignment for a client, because that's, you know, if we, it can get pretty tricky.

Like, some keywords can be a little bit misleading, and you might think you, I mean, this has also happened to me, like, I think, oh, I found, like, the perfect keyword, and then I Google it, and it's a navigational one.
Like, it's not, like, we're never going to rank for it, so you should always check the SERP for a specific keyword that you're considering.
It takes five seconds, but it's going to save you a lot of time later down the line.
Commercial keywords are okay when we're talking about blog posts that are just, like, compilation of different...
Software solutions or like alternatives, blogs.
So sometimes commercial is okay.
Sometimes there is a mix like between informational and commercial, and that's also okay for us to target, but anything transactional or navigational, we should avoid it.
It's, you know, and this is also very important to explain to our clients because sometimes they come with ideas.
They, like, want to rank for a specific keyword, and if you look up, look it up, and you see that the search intent doesn't match, we have to convey that to them.
That, like, it's, if you're looking for, like, organic traffic, it's probably not going to happen from, like, this specific assignment.
So, you know, it's important to kind of be also honest with the client if they're suggesting assignments to you.

I love zero volume keywords because those are the keywords where depending on the client and the topic, you have the opportunity to own a content category, especially with a lot of clients who are now focusing on growing conversions and leads, going after zero volume keywords or very low volume.
It's worthwhile because you have to remember that SEMrush, they use historical data.

search volume over the last 12 months.

Those keywords are completely brand new.

And from my experience, SEMrush kind of underestimates the search volume.
